response:
## Results

We evaluated ten experimental variants of the proposed method on the validation set, reporting mean accuracy and standard deviation across five random seeds for each configuration. The results are summarized as follows: variant_01 (67.2 ± 0.8), variant_02 (66.9 ± 0.7), variant_03 (67.8 ± 0.6; baseline, i.e., without the proposed component), variant_04 (65.1 ± 0.9), variant_05 (66.3 ± 1.0), variant_06 (64.7 ± 0.7), variant_07 (65.8 ± 0.8), variant_08 (70.4 ± 0.5), variant_09 (66.5 ± 0.7), and variant_10 (65.9 ± 0.8).

The baseline configuration (variant_03) achieved a mean validation accuracy of 67.8%, serving as the reference for comparison. Notably, variant_08 was the only configuration to outperform the baseline, reaching 70.4% accuracy—an absolute improvement of 2.6 percentage points. This gain is substantial given the relatively low standard deviation (±0.5), indicating consistent performance across seeds. In contrast, the remaining eight variants (variant_01, variant_02, variant_04–07, variant_09, and variant_10) all underperformed relative to the baseline, with accuracies ranging from 64.7% to 67.2%. These variants exhibited only minor differences from the baseline, with none achieving parity or superiority.

The results suggest that while most configurations of the proposed method do not yield improvements over the baseline, variant_08 demonstrates a clear and robust benefit. This highlights the importance of careful architectural or hyperparameter selection when incorporating the proposed component. Overall, the findings indicate that the proposed approach can be effective, but its success is highly dependent on the specific configuration employed.
